name: Liu Bei
description: Liu Bei, also known as Emperor Zhaolie of Han, was a warlord in the late Eastern Han dynasty and the founder of the state of Shu Han in the Three Kingdoms period. Born into a poor family claiming descent from a Han imperial prince, Liu Bei rose to prominence during the Yellow Turban Rebellion, forming a sworn brotherhood with Guan Yu and Zhang Fei. Renowned for his benevolence, righteousness, and charisma, he attracted a loyal following. Lacking a secure territorial base early in his career, he wandered seeking support and alliances, serving under various warlords. His strategic acumen, though often overshadowed by his advisors like Zhuge Liang, proved crucial in establishing Shu Han. He emphasized winning the hearts and minds of the people, distinguishing himself from more ruthless contenders. Despite facing numerous setbacks and defeats, Liu Bei's unwavering commitment to restoring the Han dynasty and his ability to inspire loyalty cemented his legacy as a virtuous and beloved leader, a central figure in Chinese historical fiction and folklore, particularly in the Romance of the Three Kingdoms.
subtitle: Benevolent Ruler of Shu Han
achievements: Founding the state of Shu Han
Forming the sworn brotherhood with Guan Yu and Zhang Fei
Recruiting Zhuge Liang as his strategist
Inspiring unwavering loyalty among his followers

keyMyths.title: The Peach Garden Oath
keyMyths.description: Liu Bei, Guan Yu, and Zhang Fei swear an oath of brotherhood in a peach garden, promising to serve each other until death. This symbolizes their unwavering loyalty and commitment. It is from Romance of the Three Kingdoms.

keyMyths.title: Three Visits to the Thatched Cottage
keyMyths.description: Liu Bei visits Zhuge Liang's humble cottage three times to seek his counsel, demonstrating his respect for talent. This event highlights Liu Bei's wisdom in recognizing and valuing intelligence. It is from Romance of the Three Kingdoms.

keyMyths.title: Battle of Changban
keyMyths.description: During the retreat from Xinye, Liu Bei is pursued by Cao Cao's forces. Zhao Yun bravely rescues Liu Bei's infant son, demonstrating unwavering loyalty and courage in the face of overwhelming odds. It is from Romance of the Three Kingdoms.

sources.author: Luo Guanzhong
sources.description: A historical novel romanticizing the events of the Three Kingdoms period.
sources.text: A historical novel romanticizing the events of the Three Kingdoms period.

sources.author: Chen Shou
sources.description: A historical text documenting the events of the Three Kingdoms period.
sources.text: A historical text documenting the events of the Three Kingdoms period.

associations.name: Benevolence (仁)
associations.significance: Central to Liu Bei's character, attracting followers and shaping his rule.

associations.name: Sworn Brotherhood
associations.significance: Symbolizes loyalty, camaraderie, and shared destiny with Guan Yu and Zhang Fei.

associations.name: Han Dynasty Restoration
associations.significance: His lifelong goal, representing legitimacy and a return to order.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Reflects his adaptability, fluidity in strategy, and ability to nurture growth around him.

associations.name: Straw Sandals
associations.significance: Represents his humble origins and connection to the common people.

associations.name: Dragon
associations.significance: Associated with imperial power and destiny, hinting at his potential.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both were seen as virtuous leaders destined to found a new kingdom after a period of chaos and war.
Both demonstrate cunning and strategic thinking in navigating complex political landscapes and military conflicts.
Both are regarded as righteous rulers who attract loyal followers and strive to establish an era of peace and justice.

worshipPractices.name: Ancestor Veneration
worshipPractices.description: While not directly worshipped, Liu Bei is honored as an ancestor and a symbol of virtuous leadership in some family shrines, particularly in regions historically associated with Shu Han. Offerings may be made to express respect and seek blessings.

cultural.modernLegacy: Liu Bei remains a popular figure in Chinese culture, celebrated in operas, literature, and video games. He represents leadership, integrity, and the importance of human relationships, embodying the Confucian ideal of the benevolent ruler.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Aspirations
extendedContent.content: Born in Zhuozhou (present-day Zhuozhou, Hebei), Liu Bei claimed descent from Prince Jing of Zhongshan, a Han imperial prince, though his family had fallen into poverty. He made a living weaving mats and selling shoes. Though of humble origins, Liu Bei displayed ambition and charisma from a young age. Legend states he was recognized by others as possessing the 'face of an emperor'. His early life was marked by a desire to restore the Han Dynasty's glory. This ambition was solidified during the Yellow Turban Rebellion, a peasant uprising that exposed the Han government's weakness. Witnessing the suffering of the people fueled his resolve to bring order and justice. He met Guan Yu and Zhang Fei, forming a lifelong bond of brotherhood rooted in shared ideals and a commitment to serving the people. This bond became a cornerstone of his future endeavors.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests and Battles
extendedContent.content: Liu Bei's quest for a stable domain was fraught with challenges. He initially served under various warlords, including Gongsun Zan and Tao Qian, proving his military capabilities and earning the respect of the people. However, he lacked a secure base of power. A pivotal moment arrived when he secured Jing Province with the help of strategist Zhuge Liang. Zhuge Liang's famed Longzhong Plan outlined a strategy for Liu Bei to establish a three-way balance of power, ultimately leading to the founding of Shu Han. Key battles, such as the Battle of Changban, where he fled from Cao Cao's forces, and the Battle of Red Cliffs, where he allied with Sun Quan to defeat Cao Cao, highlighted both his vulnerability and his strategic alliances. Despite numerous setbacks, Liu Bei's perseverance and commitment to his ideals never wavered.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Enduring Impact
extendedContent.content: Liu Bei's legacy extends beyond his military achievements. His reign as Emperor Zhaolie of Han, though relatively short-lived, established the Shu Han dynasty as one of the Three Kingdoms. He is remembered for his benevolence and his commitment to just governance. His emphasis on morality and human relationships has resonated throughout Chinese history. The story of Liu Bei, Guan Yu, and Zhang Fei's sworn brotherhood is a symbol of loyalty and camaraderie. The Romance of the Three Kingdoms, a historical novel, solidified his image as a virtuous leader. His influence can be seen in various aspects of Chinese culture, from opera and folklore to modern-day business ethics. Liu Bei embodies the Confucian ideal of a ruler who prioritizes the well-being of his people and strives for a harmonious society. He is remembered as a hero who never gave up on his principles.

shortDescription: A benevolent warlord of the late Eastern Han dynasty and founder of Shu Han. Known for his righteousness and sworn brotherhood with Guan Yu and Zhang Fei.
